title: How the body creates phantom pain in areas of past injury

htmlContent:
The ghost in the tissue

I remember the first time it happened to me. Years after a car accident that crushed my left ankle, I woke up one night certain the bone was breaking again. The pain was specific. Sharp. Familiar. I could almost feel the exact point where the fracture had been, the same grinding sensation that had sent me to the ER years before. But when I looked down, my ankle was fine. No swelling. No redness. Just my nervous system screaming about something that was no longer there.

That's phantom pain. Not the kind amputees experience in missing limbs, but the kind your body creates in places that have healed. Places where the injury is gone but the memory of it remains. And here's the thing: it's not in your head. Not really. It's in your nervous system, your brainstem, your spinal cord. It's in the architecture of how your body learned to protect itself.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching this phenomenon play out in trauma survivors. He noticed that animals in the wild rarely develop chronic pain after injury. They shake, tremble, and discharge the energy of the event. Then they walk away. But humans? We hold onto the pattern. The body doesn't always know the difference between a healed wound and a threat that never fully passed.

So when you feel that old ache in your knee, that twinge in your lower back where you pulled something ten years ago, that burning sensation in your shoulder after a surgery that "went well" - your body isn't lying to you. It's telling you the truth about what it remembers. The question is: what do we do with that truth?

Stay with me here. Because understanding how phantom pain works might be the first step toward actually letting it go.

The nervous system's map of injury

Your brain doesn't have a direct line to every cell in your body. It works through a kind of relay system, a map that gets updated based on experience. Think of it like a GPS that learns which roads are dangerous. If you hit a pothole on a certain street, your GPS flags that location. Every time you approach it, the system warns you: caution, rough road ahead.

That's what happens with past injury. The nervous system creates a kind of "danger map" around the site of trauma. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, has written extensively about how the brain tags certain body areas as high-risk zones. The tagging isn't rational. It doesn't care that the bone healed or the wound closed. It only cares about survival.

So when you move your body in a way that resembles the original injury, or when you feel stress that mirrors the emotional state you were in when it happened, the nervous system activates the pain pathway again. It's not sending a signal about current damage. It's sending a signal about potential threat. And to your brain, potential threat feels exactly like real pain.

I've worked with people who had surgery years ago and still feel the incision site burning when they're anxious. I've talked to runners whose old ankle injuries flare up only during times of emotional stress. The pattern is consistent. The body doesn't forget. But it also doesn't always distinguish between then and now.

This is where things get interesting. Because if the pain is being generated by the nervous system's map rather than by tissue damage, then changing the map might change the pain. Right?

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who integrates sensorimotor approaches, calls this "the tyranny of the past in the present." The body lives in a kind of time loop, replaying old threats as if they're happening right now. And the only way out of that loop is to help the nervous system update its map. To teach it that the danger is over. That the road is safe now.

But you can't just tell yourself that. The body doesn't respond to logic. It responds to sensation, to safety cues, to slow and repeated experiences of being okay in the places it once wasn't.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice, a book by Peter Levine that walks through exactly how the body stores and releases these patterns. It's not a quick fix. But it's a map for understanding the territory.

The role of the polyvagal system in phantom pain

Stephen Porges developed polyvagal theory, and it changed how I understand phantom pain. His work shows that the vagus nerve, which runs from your brainstem to your gut, is constantly scanning your body and environment for safety or danger. It's called neuroception. And it happens below conscious awareness.

When your nervous system detects a cue of safety, your vagal tone increases. Your heart rate slows. Your breathing deepens. Your muscles relax. But when it detects a cue of danger, even a subtle one, the system shifts into defense. And one of the ways it defends is by tensing muscles, restricting blood flow, and creating pain signals in areas that were previously injured.

Think about it. If you had a bad fall on your right hip, your nervous system might learn to brace that area whenever you feel uncertain or threatened. The bracing happens automatically. You don't even notice it. But over time, that chronic tension creates its own pain. And because the original injury site is already tagged as vulnerable, the pain gets amplified there.

So now you have a feedback loop. The original injury heals, but the bracing pattern remains. The bracing creates tension. The tension creates pain. The pain reinforces the danger signal. And the cycle continues.

Deb Dana, who writes about applying polyvagal theory in clinical practice, describes this as "the body's story." Every sensation, every ache, every twinge is part of a narrative the nervous system is telling. And that narrative is not necessarily about current reality. It's about what the body learned to expect.

So how do you interrupt the loop? You have to give the nervous system a different experience. You have to show it, through sensation, that the area is safe now. That you can move without bracing. That you can breathe without tightening. That you can rest without waiting for the other shoe to drop.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. The gentle pressure can help downregulate the threat response, which might reduce the bracing that keeps phantom pain alive. It's not a cure. But it's a tool for creating a different sensory story.

How to approach phantom pain without fighting it

Most of us respond to chronic pain by trying to eliminate it. We stretch, we massage, we take medication, we ice, we heat. We treat the pain like an enemy to be defeated. And that makes sense. Pain is unpleasant. We want it gone.

But here's the paradox: fighting phantom pain often makes it worse. Because the nervous system interprets the fight as confirmation that something is wrong. The more attention you give the pain, the more it becomes a signal of threat. And the more it becomes a signal of threat, the more the nervous system generates it.

I'm not saying ignore the pain. I'm saying approach it differently. Instead of trying to get rid of it, try to be curious about it. What shape is the pain? What color? Does it have a temperature? Does it move or stay still? When you breathe into the area, does anything shift?

This is the kind of inquiry that Peter Levine and Pat Ogden, who developed sensorimotor psychotherapy, advocate for. It's not about forcing the pain away. It's about tracking it with a gentle, non-judgmental awareness. And in that tracking, something strange often happens. The pain changes. It might fade. It might move to a different part of the body. It might intensify briefly and then release.

The goal isn't to make the pain disappear. The goal is to change your relationship to it. To stop treating it as a signal of danger and start treating it as a signal of something that needs to be heard. Because phantom pain is often the body's way of saying: I never fully processed what happened here. I'm still holding onto the energy of that moment. Can you help me let it go?

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger, which lays out the principles of trauma release in a way that's both practical and . It's not a manual. It's more like a guide to understanding why your body does what it does.

I also find that reducing sensory overload can help the nervous system settle enough to process old pain. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. When your auditory system isn't being bombarded, your nervous system can sometimes shift toward safety more easily. It's a small thing. But small things add up.

What phantom pain teaches us about healing

I don't think phantom pain is a mistake. I think it's a message. It's the body's way of telling us that healing isn't just about tissue repair. It's about the whole story. The emotional context. The unresolved fear. The parts of the experience that never got to move through.

Gabor Mate talks about this constantly. He says that trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. And that internal response gets stored in the body. It becomes the basis for future responses. It shapes how you move, how you breathe, how you hold yourself in the world.

So when phantom pain shows up, it's not a failure of healing. It's a reminder that healing is ongoing. That the body is still working on integrating the experience. That there's more to process, more to release, more to come back to.

And that's okay. You don't have to rush it. You don't have to fix it overnight. You just have to stay in relationship with your body, even when it hurts. Even when the pain doesn't make sense. Even when you're tired of feeling things that shouldn't be there.

Sit with that for a moment. Notice what just happened in your body. Maybe you tensed up. Maybe you relaxed a little. Maybe you felt nothing at all. Whatever it was, it's information. And information is the first step toward change.

The phantom pain isn't the enemy. It's the messenger. And if you can learn to listen to it without fear, something might shift. Not because you fought the pain. But because you finally heard what it was trying to say.
